title: Dirty rice
subtitle: Cajun rice cooked with chicken livers, the holy trinity, and spices
excerpt: Louisiana Cajun dirty rice: long-grain white rice cooked with minced chicken livers, the classic combination of onion, celery, and green pepper, and a seasoning of cayenne, smoked paprika, and thyme. The chicken livers give the rice its characteristic dark colour and savoury, earthy depth.
sourceNotes: Dirty rice is a Cajun dish associated with Louisiana, documented in American regional cookery literature from the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The name refers to the colour the chicken livers and spices give the rice. Multiple cross-referenced Louisiana Cajun cookery sources.

content.text: The chicken livers are the key ingredient and the source of the dish's name. They are cooked until no pink remains, then chopped finely so they blend into the rice rather than appearing as distinct pieces. If you have not cooked chicken livers before, the smell while cooking is strong. It mellows once the livers are done and the spices go in. Do not skip the livers; there is no substitute that produces the same depth of flavour.

The rice is cooked absorption-style in chicken stock after the aromatics are softened. The stock absorbs the rendered fat and the spice flavours, so the rice ends up tasting of everything that went in before it.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil in a large heavy pan over high heat. Add the {{chicken-livers}} of chicken livers and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, turning once, until browned outside and just cooked through with no pink remaining. Remove and chop finely. Reduce the heat to medium. In the same pan, cook the {{onion}} onion, {{celery}} celery, and {{pepper-green}} green pepper for 8 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cayenne}} of cayenne, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{thyme-dried}} of dried thyme. Stir for 1 minute. Return the chopped livers to the pan.

Add the {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice and stir to coat in the spiced fat. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Season with salt and pepper. Bring to the boil, then reduce the heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly, and cook for 18 minutes. Remove from the heat and leave to steam, covered, for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Scatter over the {{spring-onion}} spring onions and serve.

items.fix: Measure the stock accurately; once covered, do not lift the lid until the 18 minutes are up
items.cause: Too much liquid, or lid lifted during cooking

items.fix: Cook over high heat for 3 to 4 minutes only; cut one piece open, no pink, but still moist inside is the target
items.cause: Overcooked, livers become grainy and chalky past the point of being just cooked through
items.symptom: Chicken livers are grainy and dry

items.fix: Chop the cooked livers very finely, almost to a paste, before returning to the pan; they should season the rice, not dominate it
items.symptom: Rice has a strong liver taste throughout

content.text: Dirty rice is a Cajun staple, made wherever there are chicken livers and leftover rice. The name is direct, the livers and spices make the rice look dirty, and the dish does not attempt to disguise this. It is a side dish alongside fried chicken or grilled meats, or eaten on its own as a simple main. In Louisiana it appears at home tables, church suppers, and diners, often made in large quantities and reheated through the week. The holy trinity base (onion, celery, green pepper) is the foundation it shares with gumbo, étouffée, and most other Cajun and Creole dishes.
